short_putt_misses.text: I'm playing in more tournaments this year and one of the problems I'm having is struggling with short putts while in competition. I start missing 2-4 footers, usually by pulling them and once it starts, it continues.

Jeez yeah. I had a two footer the other day in a club tourney. I set up, and knew with conviction that I was going to miss it. I didn't know what to do at the time except try to make it. Of course I missed it, a pull.
Very interested in this. I almost made the same post yesterday. My miss tends to be right though. Two weeks ago in a comp I missed two 2 foot putts and lost by a tiebreaker. That one hurt a lot.

three_putting.text: Just got to rant for a second, hopefully, this will make me feel better. My last few rounds go like this: 92 with Forty-F*cking-Two putts, that's right 42! Which includes a 4 putt....wtf!?
I'm definitely subscribing to this thread. I'm in the exact same boat. I have confidence in the 4 footers. Everything outside of that, I just plan on the 2 putt and just try to avoid the three.

yips.text: Hey guys, need some help with my putting yips. So my yips started about 7 years ago. Basically, whenever I put in pressure situations (mostly 3-4 footers), my right hand will twitch.

Hi.. anyone really cure putting yips and now putt fine now conventionally? To me, changes like saw grip, long putter, putting left handed, etc aren't cures, they are just work arounds and less effective approaches.
I got them so bad a few years ago that I could actually miss the ball when putting. I almost quit golf.

confidence_loss.text: I need to go see a shrink! For the past 15 years of playing this silly game I've never been happy with my putting. My woods and irons are very solid but as soon as I get the putter in my hands all of my confidence goes out the door no matter how far away from the cup I am.

face_control_issues.text: One of my hunches is that I take too long of a back swing on the real short putts. So I'm probably decelerating and that probably leads to early release and closed face i.e. pulls.
face_control_issues.mechanism_insight: Deceleration causes face to close before impact

face_control_issues.text: My flinch is a hold on with my left hand. This leaves the face wide open.
face_control_issues.mechanism_insight: Tension in dominant hand causes face to open

face_control_issues.text: Too much thinking brings on pressure. We get nervous and slow down the stroke trying to be perfect. We baby the putt, letting the natural rotation/release start to dominate and we get a weak pull.
face_control_issues.mechanism_insight: Natural rotation causes face to close when decelerating

stroke_mechanics.text: I lacked confidence in my putting because too many putts just didn't fall -- but they weren't falling because I had too many variables in my stroke.
stroke_mechanics.mechanism_insight: Stroke inconsistency leads to confidence loss

stroke_mechanics.text: I also noticed that after 100s of hours of practice that my stroke was way too inside. If I stroke more outside the line, at least its the feeling, my stroke is much smoother and straighter and yips are very minimal.
stroke_mechanics.mechanism_insight: Inside stroke path causes face control issues

alignment_setup.text: I didn't realize my set up was a bit open - shoulders and hips - and a line from both elbows were almost 45 degrees open of my target, accounting for my coming over the top and missing left.
alignment_setup.mechanism_insight: Open alignment causes compensations and pulls

alignment_setup.text: 1 inch variance in ball position can lead to a 1 foot right/left variance at 10 feet...so at 3 feet that's about 4 inches either way - might lead to a lot burned edges or lip outs at 3 feet.
alignment_setup.mechanism_insight: Small setup inconsistencies cause large directional errors

mental_factors.text: Pulls are frequently caused by peeking early to see if you made the putt. Also peeking early occurs when you are trying to see break for the comeback putt should you miss. The more meaningful a putt, the more likely to be anxious to see and confirm the result.
mental_factors.mechanism_insight: Anxiety causes early head movement, disrupting stroke

mental_factors.text: A lot of times you see guys who are struggling with their putting miss all their putts but make the putts they don't bother trying to make. That successful putt was totally done by feel whereas the putts they try to make are screwed up because their routine eliminates all their natural feel for putting.
mental_factors.mechanism_insight: Overthinking eliminates natural feel and rhythm

grip_changes.text: Cure? No, I don't think anyone really cures the yips. Get them under control - Yes. Had the yips at least 4 times in my days. First time around I got it under control by controlling my thought process on what was makeable and what was reasonable.

Getting it under control is the goal. I've combated it with moving the ball up in my stance, and taking my left thumb off the grip and placing it over my right hand. This makes a right hand only stroke.

equipment_changes.text: I've tried: LAB putters (gripping very lightly), arm-lock (hurts my left wrist, uncomfortable), face-balance, toe hang, and everything in between, different putter grips (smaller, thicker).

stroke_modifications.text: I've adopted more of a pop stroke when putting inside 6 feet. I tend to pull on shorter putts with a normal full stroke, the pop stroke gives it enough energy to get to the hole and because the stroke stops at the point I contact the ball I don't feel any chance of pulling the putt.
A big change a number of years ago was changing the stroke pace to one that does not accelerate through the ball. Smooth back. Smooth through. Speed is based on length of swing.

LAB_Golf.text: I got my DF3 about a month and a half ago. For the first time possibly ever… I don't have the urge to keep chasing that feeling anymore. I've sold off other putters. I stopped going to my local big box store to try out new releases.
Same. I used to have 30+ putters (a Lamb, Scottys, Piretti, Bettis, ect). I sold them all off.

I picked up a DF3 recently and it's an excellent putter. I also realize there are things more important to me than putting well, namely buying good looking putters. DF3 makes putts sure but it's not something I like to hold and look at.

recovery_stories.text: I do know a guy who went from 2 to 9 and couldn't hit a 6 inch putt. It took 4 years, but he is back to a 2 and putting well without any changes, so it can be done.

face_control_dominant_problem.description: Face opening/closing during stroke is the most commonly cited root cause of putting issues
face_control_dominant_problem.rs1_relevance: HIGH - RS1's Forward Axis Balance directly addresses face stability

equipment_changes_dont_work.description: Golfers report trying multiple putters without success before finding solution
equipment_changes_dont_work.rs1_relevance: HIGH - Demonstrates need for mechanism-based solution, not just different putter

deceleration_causes_face_rotation.description: When golfers decelerate through impact, natural hand rotation causes face to close
deceleration_causes_face_rotation.rs1_relevance: HIGH - Forward balance keeps face stable even with tempo variations

yips_connected_to_face_uncertainty.description: Many yips cases stem from uncertainty about face position at impact
yips_connected_to_face_uncertainty.rs1_relevance: HIGH - Consistent face position reduces mental anxiety
